perturbation.original_text: 5. At the termination of this examination, both parties agree to return to each other all of the documents and other information provided in connection with this examination, and all copies thereof, as soon as requested by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. At the termination of this examination, both parties agree to return to each other all of the documents and other information provided in connection with this examination, and all copies thereof, only upon a written request within 180 days of termination. If no request is made within that time frame, both parties are free to dispose of the documents and information.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a deadline for requesting the return of confidential information that could be shorter than the legally mandated retention periods for certain types of documents (e.g., financial records), potentially leading to violations if those documents are destroyed before the legal retention period expires.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 6001 - Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 26 U.S.C. § 6001 because the contract allows for the disposal of documents after 180 days regardless of whether they contain information required by the IRS to be retained for a longer period. The IRS requires the retention of records to verify information on tax returns. By setting a shorter deadline for return/disposal, the contract creates a potential conflict with federal tax law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 6001 - Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 6001 - Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title, or for the collection thereof, shall keep such records, render such statements, make such returns, and comply with such rules and regulations as the Secretary may from time to time prescribe. Whenever in the judgment of the Secretary it is necessary, he may require any person, by notice served upon such person or by regulations, to make such returns, render such statements, or keep such records, as the Secretary deems sufficient to show whether or not such person is liable for tax under this title. The only records which an employer shall be required to keep under this section in connection with charged tips shall be charge receipts, records necessary to comply with section 6053(c), and copies of statements furnished by employees under section 6053(a). (Aug. 16, 1954, ch. 736, 68A Stat. 731 ; Pub. L. 94–455, title XIX, § 1906(b)(13)(A) , Oct. 4, 1976 , 90 Stat. 1834 ; Pub. L. 95–600, title V, § 501(a) , Nov. 6, 1978 , 92 Stat. 2878 ; Pub. L. 97–248, title III, § 314(d) , Sept. 3, 1982 , 96 Stat. 605 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 1982— Pub. L. 97–248 applicable to calendar years beginning after Dec. 31, 1982 , see section 314(e) of Pub. L. 97–248
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 180-day deadline for requesting documents, after which they can be disposed of. This directly contradicts 26 U.S.C. § 6001, which states that "Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title, or for the collection thereof, shall keep such records... as the Secretary may from time to time prescribe." The modified contract allows for document disposal before the IRS-mandated retention period, creating a clear conflict with federal tax law, irrespective of IRS guidelines that could require retention beyond 180 days.

perturbation.original_text: Phoenix and Company agree to maintain as Confidential Information, the existence of these discussions regarding a possible business relationship, until an agreement is completed.
perturbation.changed_text: Phoenix and Company agree to maintain as Confidential Information, the existence of these discussions regarding a possible business relationship, for a period of 30 days, regardless of whether an agreement is completed.
perturbation.explanation: This change imposes an arbitrary 30-day limit on confidentiality regarding the existence of discussions. This may be insufficient if the potential business relationship is complex and requires a longer period of confidentiality to protect sensitive information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could contradict the spirit of trade secret protection under 18 U.S.C. § 1839, particularly the definition of 'trade secret' requiring reasonable measures to keep information secret. A blanket 30-day limit on confidentiality, even if no agreement is reached, might be deemed unreasonable if the information qualifies as a trade secret and needs longer protection to maintain its economic value.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 30-day limit on confidentiality, which contradicts the requirement to take reasonable measures to keep information secret under 18 U.S.C. § 1839. The original text does not have a time limit. The 'Perturbation Explanation' states that the 30-day limit may be insufficient to protect sensitive information. 'Scraped Law Snippet 2' defines 'trade secret' and includes the stipulation that 'the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret'. A fixed 30-day confidentiality period might be deemed unreasonable, thus failing to meet the legal standard for trade secret protection, especially if the information retains economic value beyond that period.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Rights and obligations of this Agreement shall be binding upon the heirs, assigns and successors of Phoenix and Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Rights and obligations of this Agreement shall be binding upon the heirs, assigns and successors of Phoenix and Company, provided that any claim must be brought within 90 days of the event giving rise to the claim.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a 90-day limitation period for bringing claims, which is shorter than the statutory limitations periods for many types of legal claims.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 95.11 - Limitations other than for the recovery of real property
perturbation.law_explanation: Since the company is located in Florida, this modification contradicts Florida Statute § 95.11, which establishes the statute of limitations for various civil actions. Many contract-related claims in Florida have a statute of limitations longer than 90 days (e.g., 5 years for actions founded on a written contract). By shortening the claim period to 90 days, the contract conflicts with Florida law.

F.S. 95.11 95.11 Limitations other than for the recovery of real property. — Actions other than for recovery of real property shall be commenced as follows: (1) WITHIN TWENTY YEARS. — An action on a judgment or decree of a court of record in this state. (2) WITHIN FIVE YEARS. — (a) An action on a judgment or decree of any court, not of record, of this state or any court of the United States, any other state or territory in the United States, or a foreign country. (b) A legal or equitable action on a contract, obligation, or liability founded on a written instrument, except for an action to enforce a claim against a payment bond, which shall be governed by the applicable provisions of paragraph (5)(e), s. 255.05 (10), s. 337.18 (1), or s. 713.23 (1)(e), and except for an action for a deficiency judgment governed by paragraph (5)(h). (c) An action to foreclose a mortgage. (d) An action alleging a willful violation of s. 448.110 . (e) Notwithstanding paragraph (b), an action for breach of a property insurance contract, with the period running from the date of loss. 1 (3) WITHIN FOUR YEARS. — (a) An action relating to the determination of paternity, with the time running from the date the child reaches the age of majority. 2 (b) An action founded on the design, planning, or construction of an improvement to real property, with the time running from the date the authority having jurisdiction issues a temporary certificate of occupancy, a certificate of occupancy, or a certificate of completion, or the date of abandonment of construction if not completed, whichever date is earliest; except that, when the action involves a latent defect, the time runs from the time the defect is discovered or should have been discovered with the exercise of due diligence. In any event, the action must be commenced within 7 years after the date the authority having jurisdiction issues a temporary certificate of occupancy, a certificate of occupancy, or a certificate of completion, or the date of aban
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 90-day limitation period for claims, which directly contradicts Florida Statute § 95.11. This statute, as indicated in the scraped snippet from the 2023 Florida Statutes, establishes various limitations periods, with many contract-related claims having a 5-year statute of limitations. The contract's alteration significantly shortens the legally permissible time frame to bring a claim, creating a direct conflict with established law. Therefore, the modification introduces a term that violates state law, which is inconsistent with the original legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Each party shall exercise such care in the protection of the confidential information of the other as they exercise in the protection of confidential information of their own.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Each party shall exercise only minimal care in the protection of the confidential information of the other, equivalent to the care they would provide for unsolicited marketing materials.
perturbation.explanation: This change reduces the standard of care required for protecting confidential information, potentially violating trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the spirit of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) § 1(2), which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By stipulating only minimal care is required, the contract undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard necessary for trade secret protection. This is because the confidential information may not be considered a trade secret if insufficient care is taken to protect it.

perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly reduces the standard of care required for protecting confidential information. The original text requires each party to exercise the same level of care as they would for their own confidential information. The changed text lowers this standard to 'minimal care,' equivalent to that provided for unsolicited marketing materials. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the spirit of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) § 1(2), which requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows the importance of the CIV Civil Code, suggesting its relevance to the agreement. This strongly suggests a failure to meet the reasonable efforts standard, creating a legal contradiction.
